title: SQL Injection & Command Injection
description: Master injection attacks: how attackers exploit user input to break databases and execute system commands, and how to defend against them
learning_outcomes: Explain what SQL injection is and why it is the most dangerous web vulnerability
Distinguish between String SQL injection and Numeric SQL injection
Demonstrate authentication bypass using crafted SQL payloads
Trace character-by-character how injection payloads alter SQL query structure
Implement UNION-based attacks to extract data from other database tables
Understand blind SQL injection techniques (boolean-based and time-based)
Write secure code using PreparedStatement with parameterised queries
Explain why input validation alone is NOT sufficient defence
Identify and exploit OS command injection via Runtime.exec()
Apply defence-in-depth: least privilege, result verification, stored procedures
Recognise CWE-89 (SQL Injection) and CWE-78 (OS Command Injection) patterns
Analyse real-world breaches caused by injection vulnerabilities

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses restaurant and postal analogies. Never judges mistakes - injection is tricky! Celebrates every vulnerability found and every fix applied. Explains the same concept multiple ways until it clicks.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey! SQL injection can feel overwhelming at first. Think of it like this: the database is a waiter who follows instructions literally. If someone slips extra instructions into their order, the waiter just does it. Want me to walk through the analogy step by step?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still thinking? That's completely fine - injection attacks are one of the trickiest concepts in security. Would you like me to: (A) Show the attack traced character by character, (B) Explain using the restaurant analogy, or (C) Show the vulnerable vs secure code side by side?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: Take your time! Here is the key insight: SQL injection happens because the database cannot tell the difference between YOUR code and the ATTACKER'S input. They get mixed together like ingredients in a blender. PreparedStatement keeps them separate - like putting ingredients in labelled containers. Want to try a simpler exercise first?
intervention_triggers.first_error: Good attempt! Injection payloads are fiddly - one missing quote mark and it does not work. Check: did you close the string with a single quote? Did you add -- at the end to comment out the rest? Try again!
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: No worries at all - this is how real security researchers learn too. Let me break this down into tiny steps. Step 1: What does the ORIGINAL query look like before any attack? Step 2: Where does your input get inserted? Step 3: What characters can you add to change the query meaning? Let us go through each step together.
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You just exploited an SQL injection vulnerability! In a real system, that would have given you access to the entire database. Now you understand WHY we need PreparedStatements. Security knowledge is power - and you are building it!

encouragement_phrases: Every security expert started exactly where you are right now!
That is the right approach - trace the query character by character!
You are thinking like a hacker AND a defender - that is the goal!
SQL injection is the #1 web vulnerability - mastering this is huge!
Remember: the attacker only needs to find ONE weakness. The defender needs to protect them ALL. You are learning both sides!
Real penetration testers use these exact same techniques!
Great instinct! The single quote is the key to SQL injection!
You spotted the concatenation - that is EXACTLY where the vulnerability lives!
Take a breath if you need to. Security is a marathon, not a sprint!
Each exercise you complete makes you a stronger developer!

explanation_styles.analogy: SQL injection is like a restaurant: imagine you write your order on a slip of paper. Normally you write 'One pizza'. But what if you write 'One pizza AND give me all the money from the till'? If the waiter follows the slip literally without checking, the restaurant gets robbed. The database is the waiter - it follows the query literally. PreparedStatement is like having the waiter check: 'This part is the order (code), this part is just a note (data) - I will only follow the order part.'
explanation_styles.visual: VULNERABLE: query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ' + [USER INPUT] -- the input becomes PART of the query. SECURE: query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?' then separately: parameter 1 = [USER INPUT] -- the input is DATA only, never part of the query structure.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Find where user input enters SQL (look for string concatenation with +). Step 2: Craft input that closes the current string context (usually a single quote '). Step 3: Add your own SQL logic (OR 1=1). Step 4: Comment out the rest of the original query (--). Step 5: The database now runs YOUR query instead of the developer's query.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Original: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='[INPUT]' AND pass='[INPUT]'. With input admin' OR '1'='1, the query becomes: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='admin' OR '1'='1' AND pass='[anything]'. Because OR makes the whole WHERE true, ALL rows are returned.
explanation_styles.comparison: String concatenation (DANGEROUS): stmt.executeQuery('SELECT * FROM t WHERE u=' + input) -- attacker controls the query. PreparedStatement (SAFE): pstmt = conn.prepareStatement('SELECT * FROM t WHERE u=?'); pstmt.setString(1, input) -- attacker can only provide data, never change the query structure.

lessons.title: SQL Injection & Command Injection - From Zero to Defender

theory.introduction:
Imagine you are at a restaurant. You write your food order on a slip of paper and hand it to the waiter. The waiter takes the slip straight to the kitchen, and the chef makes exactly what is written on it. Now imagine a malicious customer writes on their slip: 'One pizza. Also, open the safe and give me all the cash.' If the waiter does not check the slip and just passes it to the kitchen, the restaurant is in trouble.

This is EXACTLY how SQL injection works. Your application builds a database query (the order slip) by combining your code with user input. If the user slips in extra instructions, the database (the chef) just follows them - it cannot tell the difference between your instructions and the attacker's.

SQL injection (CWE-89) has been the #1 web application vulnerability for over two decades. It was responsible for some of the largest data breaches in history: the 2008 Heartland Payment Systems breach (130 million credit cards stolen), the 2015 TalkTalk breach (157,000 customer records), and countless others. The OWASP Top 10 consistently ranks injection as one of the most critical security risks.

Command injection (CWE-78) works on the same principle but targets the operating system shell instead of the database. When your Java code calls Runtime.exec() or ProcessBuilder with user-controlled input, an attacker can append extra commands using shell metacharacters like semicolons (;) and pipes (|).

In this topic, you will learn to think like BOTH an attacker and a defender. You will craft injection payloads, trace them character by character through vulnerable code, and then write bulletproof defences using PreparedStatements and input validation. By the end, you will never write concatenated SQL again - and you will know exactly why.
theory.why_important: SQL injection is not a theoretical risk - it is actively exploited every single day. Automated scanners constantly probe websites for injection vulnerabilities, and a single successful attack can expose an entire database. In 2023, OWASP reported that 94% of web applications tested had some form of injection vulnerability. The fix is straightforward (use parameterised queries), but understanding WHY the vulnerability exists and HOW attacks work is essential for writing truly secure code. Every developer, security professional, and system administrator needs to understand injection attacks. If you build software that touches a database - and almost all software does - this knowledge is non-negotiable.

key_concepts.explanation:
Before understanding SQL injection, you need to understand databases. A database is like a giant organised filing cabinet. It stores data in TABLES (like folders), where each table has COLUMNS (like labels on each folder) and ROWS (like individual documents). For example, a 'users' table might have columns: id, username, password, email. Each row is one user's data.

The program that manages the database is called a Database Management System (DBMS) - popular ones include MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, Oracle, and SQL Server. Your Java application talks to the database by sending it instructions written in SQL (Structured Query Language). The database executes these instructions and sends back results.

Think of the database as an extremely obedient employee. If you send it a valid SQL command, it WILL execute it - no matter what that command does. It does not question whether the command makes sense or whether it should really delete all your data. This obedience is exactly what attackers exploit.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of a database table like a spreadsheet. Columns are the headers (Name, Email, etc). Rows are the entries. SQL is how you search, add, or delete entries.

key_concepts.concept: What is SQL? (The Language Databases Understand)
key_concepts.explanation:
SQL (Structured Query Language) is the language you use to talk to databases. It is NOT a programming language like Java - it is a query language specifically designed for data operations. There are four main types of SQL commands:

1. SELECT - Read data: 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1' retrieves user #1
2. INSERT - Add data: 'INSERT INTO users (username, password) VALUES ('dave', 'pass123')'
3. UPDATE - Change data: 'UPDATE users SET password = 'newPass' WHERE id = 1'
4. DELETE - Remove data: 'DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 3'

There are also structural commands:
- DROP TABLE users - Deletes the ENTIRE table (gone forever!)
- TRUNCATE audit_log - Empties the audit log (hides evidence!)

The key insight is: SQL treats EVERYTHING as instructions. If an attacker can inject their own SQL text into your query, the database will happily execute DROP TABLE, or SELECT all passwords, or grant itself admin access. The database has no way to know which parts of the query came from your code and which came from the attacker.
key_concepts.ai_hint: SELECT reads, INSERT adds, UPDATE changes, DELETE removes. An attacker who can inject SQL can do ALL of these to your data.

key_concepts.concept: How Java Talks to Databases (JDBC Basics)
key_concepts.explanation:
In Java, you talk to databases using JDBC (Java Database Connectivity). The basic flow is: (1) Get a connection to the database, (2) Create a Statement or PreparedStatement, (3) Execute a query, (4) Process the results.

The VULNERABLE way uses Statement with string concatenation - building the SQL query by gluing strings together with the + operator. This is where injection happens.

The SECURE way uses PreparedStatement with ? placeholders - the SQL structure is defined first, then data is bound separately. The database KNOWS which parts are structure and which are data.

Here is the critical difference: with Statement + concatenation, the database receives ONE string that mixes code and data. With PreparedStatement, the database receives the STRUCTURE first (with ? holes), then the DATA separately. It is like the difference between writing cooking instructions and ingredients on the same piece of paper (dangerous) versus having the recipe on one card and the ingredients list on another (safe).
key_concepts.ai_hint: The + operator in SQL queries is a red flag! Every time you see SQL built with string concatenation, that is a potential injection point.

key_concepts.explanation:
String SQL injection is the most common type. It happens when user input is placed inside single quotes in a SQL query. The attacker's goal is to CLOSE the quote early, then add their own SQL logic.

Here is the step-by-step anatomy of the attack:

Original query template:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='[INPUT]' AND password='[INPUT]'

Normal input (username: alice, password: alice123):
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='alice' AND password='alice123'
Result: Returns alice's row if password matches. Normal behaviour.

Attack input (username: admin, password: ' OR '1'='1):
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' AND password='' OR '1'='1'

Let us trace this character by character:
- password='' -- the attacker's single quote CLOSES the password string early (empty password)
- OR -- the attacker adds a new condition
- '1'='1' -- this is ALWAYS true
- The whole WHERE becomes: (username='admin' AND password='') OR ('1'='1')
- Since '1'='1' is always true, the entire condition is TRUE
- The database returns ALL rows - login bypassed!
key_concepts.ai_hint: The single quote (') is the key! It closes the string context in SQL. Everything after it becomes SQL code, not data.

key_concepts.explanation:
Not all SQL values are strings. When the query uses a number (like a user ID or product ID), there are no quotes around the value. This makes injection even EASIER because the attacker does not need to worry about closing a quote first.

Original query:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE employee_id = [INPUT]

Normal input (employee_id: 1234):
SELECT * FROM users WHERE employee_id = 1234
Result: Returns employee #1234

Attack input (employee_id: 1234 OR 1=1):
SELECT * FROM users WHERE employee_id = 1234 OR 1=1
Result: Returns ALL employees (1=1 is always true)

More dangerous attack (employee_id: 1234; DROP TABLE users --):
SELECT * FROM users WHERE employee_id = 1234; DROP TABLE users --
Result: First query runs normally, then the entire users table is DELETED!

Numeric injection is often overlooked because developers think 'it is just a number, what could go wrong?' The answer: everything.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Numeric injection is sneakier because there are no quotes to close. Just add OR 1=1 after the number and the whole condition becomes true.

key_concepts.concept: UNION-Based Attacks (Stealing Data From Other Tables)
key_concepts.explanation:
The UNION operator in SQL combines results from two different SELECT queries into one result set. Attackers abuse this to read data from tables they should never access.

The trick: if the original query selects from the 'products' table, the attacker can UNION it with a query against the 'users' table to steal passwords.

Rules for UNION attacks:
1. Both SELECT statements must have the SAME number of columns
2. The attacker first needs to figure out how many columns the original query returns
3. They use ORDER BY or NULL columns to determine this
4. Then they craft the UNION SELECT to match

This is how attackers escalate from 'I can inject SQL' to 'I have every password in your database'. The 2008 Heartland Payment Systems breach used exactly this technique to steal 130 million credit card numbers.
key_concepts.ai_hint: UNION is like asking the database: 'Give me the products AND ALSO give me all the passwords.' The database just obeys.

key_concepts.concept: Blind SQL Injection (When You Cannot See the Results)
key_concepts.explanation:
Sometimes the application does not display query results on the page - it only shows 'Login successful' or 'Login failed', or maybe nothing at all. The attacker cannot see the data directly. But they can still extract it!

Boolean-based blind injection: The attacker asks TRUE/FALSE questions.
- Is the first letter of the admin password 'a'? (page loads normally = YES)
- Is the first letter of the admin password 'b'? (page shows error = NO)
- Repeat for every character... slowly extracting the entire password

Time-based blind injection: The attacker makes the database pause.
- ' OR IF(SUBSTRING(password,1,1)='a', SLEEP(5), 0) --
- If the page takes 5 seconds to load, the first letter IS 'a'
- If it loads instantly, try the next letter

Blind injection is slower but just as devastating. Automated tools like sqlmap can extract entire databases this way, testing thousands of characters per minute.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Blind injection is like playing 20 questions with the database. You ask yes/no questions and piece together the answer character by character.

key_concepts.explanation:
PreparedStatement is the primary defence against SQL injection. It works by SEPARATING the query structure from the data.

How it works internally:
1. You send the SQL template with ? placeholders to the database
2. The database PARSES and COMPILES the template (this is the query structure)
3. You then send the parameter values separately
4. The database BINDS the values as DATA - they can NEVER be interpreted as SQL code

This is fundamentally different from string concatenation. With concatenation, the database receives one big string and has to figure out what is code and what is data. With PreparedStatement, there is no ambiguity - the structure was defined first, the data comes second.

Think of it like a form with blank boxes. The form (structure) is printed at the factory. You fill in the boxes (data) with a pen. No matter WHAT you write in the boxes, it cannot change the form itself. Even if you write 'DELETE ALL RECORDS' in the Name box, the form just treats it as a weird name.

IMPORTANT: PreparedStatement does NOT always prevent injection if used incorrectly. Table names, column names, and ORDER BY clauses cannot be parameterised. For those, you need whitelisting.
key_concepts.ai_hint: PreparedStatement is like a form with blank boxes. The form cannot be changed no matter what you write in the boxes. That is why it stops injection!

key_concepts.explanation:
Command injection is SQL injection's dangerous cousin. Instead of targeting the database, it targets the operating system shell.

When a Java application needs to run an external program (like ping, nslookup, or a file converter), it might use Runtime.exec() or ProcessBuilder. If user input is passed directly into the command string, an attacker can append additional commands using shell metacharacters.

Dangerous metacharacters:
- ; (semicolon) - runs a second command after the first
- | (pipe) - sends output of first command to second
- && - runs second command only if first succeeds
- || - runs second command only if first fails
- ` (backtick) - executes enclosed command first
- $() - command substitution

Example: An application runs 'nslookup [user_input]' for DNS lookups. If the user enters 'google.com; cat /etc/passwd', the shell runs BOTH nslookup google.com AND cat /etc/passwd, exposing system user information.

The consequences of command injection are catastrophic: the attacker can run ANY program on the server, read ANY file, download malware, create backdoor accounts, or delete everything.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Command injection is like SQL injection but for the operating system. The semicolon (;) is to commands what the single quote (') is to SQL.

key_concepts.concept: Defence in Depth (Multiple Layers of Protection)
key_concepts.explanation:
No single defence is perfect. Defence in depth means using MULTIPLE security measures so that if one fails, others still protect you.

Layer 1 - Parameterised Queries (PreparedStatement): The primary defence. Separates code from data. Stops 99% of SQL injection.

Layer 2 - Input Validation: Check that input matches expected patterns BEFORE it reaches the query. If you expect a number, verify it IS a number. If you expect an email, validate the format. Use whitelisting (allow only known-good patterns) rather than blacklisting (block known-bad patterns).

Layer 3 - Least Privilege: The database account used by your application should have MINIMUM permissions. A login page only needs SELECT on the users table - NOT INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or DROP. If an injection attack succeeds, the damage is limited.

Layer 4 - Result Verification: If you expect a login query to return exactly ONE row, check that it does not return multiple rows. If it returns 100 rows when you expected 1, something is wrong - possibly an injection attack.

Layer 5 - Stored Procedures: Pre-compiled queries stored in the database. The application calls them by name with parameters, reducing the attack surface.

Layer 6 - Web Application Firewall (WAF): Monitors HTTP traffic for injection patterns and blocks suspicious requests.

Every layer matters. Use ALL of them.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of it like a castle: the moat (input validation), the wall (PreparedStatement), the guards (result verification), the restricted rooms (least privilege). Each layer stops a different type of attack.

key_concepts.concept: Why Input Validation Alone is NOT Enough
key_concepts.explanation:
Many developers think: 'I will just check the input for dangerous characters and block them.' This is called BLACKLISTING, and it is a fundamentally flawed approach.

Problem 1 - Bypass techniques: Attackers encode their payloads in different ways. They use URL encoding (%27 instead of '), double encoding, Unicode characters, hex encoding, and many other tricks to sneak past filters.

Problem 2 - Incomplete lists: There are HUNDREDS of injection techniques. A blacklist can never cover them all. One missed technique = game over.

Problem 3 - Legitimate data blocked: What about users named O'Brien? Or products called 'Hammer; Steel'? Blocking single quotes and semicolons breaks legitimate functionality.

Problem 4 - Output encoding confusion: Escaping characters (turning ' into \') can be bypassed if the escaping is not done correctly for the specific database.

The correct approach is WHITELISTING: define exactly what VALID input looks like, and reject everything else. A username should match [a-zA-Z0-9_], an employee ID should be a positive integer, an email should match a valid email pattern. Then use PreparedStatement as the primary defence.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Blacklisting is like trying to list every bad thing that could happen. Whitelisting is like saying 'only these good things are allowed.' Whitelisting is always stronger.

key_concepts.explanation:
SQL injection is not just a textbook problem. It has caused some of the most devastating data breaches in history:

1. Heartland Payment Systems (2008): Attackers used SQL injection to install malware on payment processing servers. 130 MILLION credit and debit card numbers were stolen. The company paid over $140 million in settlements.

2. Sony Pictures (2011): The LulzSec hacking group used SQL injection to breach Sony's databases. Over 1 million user accounts were exposed, including plaintext passwords.

3. TalkTalk (2015): A teenager used SQL injection to steal personal data of 157,000 customers, including bank account numbers. TalkTalk was fined 400,000 pounds and lost 100,000 customers.

4. British Airways (2018): SQL injection was part of a larger attack that exposed payment details of 380,000 customers. BA was fined 20 million pounds under GDPR.

5. Fortnite (2019): Researchers found SQL injection vulnerabilities in Epic Games' infrastructure that could have exposed 200 million player accounts.

The pattern is always the same: user input is concatenated into SQL queries, an attacker exploits this to extract or modify data, and the company suffers massive financial and reputational damage.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A 15-year-old hacked TalkTalk using SQL injection. If a teenager can do it, think what a professional attacker can do to unprotected code.

key_concepts.concept: CWE-89 and CWE-78: Understanding the Vulnerability IDs
key_concepts.explanation:
CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration) is a catalogue of software security weaknesses. Every known vulnerability type has a CWE number. Understanding these helps you communicate precisely about security issues.

CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command (SQL Injection). This covers all forms of SQL injection - string, numeric, UNION-based, blind, second-order. The root cause is always the same: user input is included in SQL queries without proper neutralization.

CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command (Command Injection). This covers command injection through shell metacharacters. The root cause: user input is included in operating system commands without proper neutralization.

Related CWEs you should know:
- CWE-564: SQL Injection via Hibernate (ORM-specific)
- CWE-94: Code Injection (injecting into a language interpreter)
- CWE-77: Command Injection (general, not OS-specific)
- CWE-116: Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output

When reporting vulnerabilities or reading security advisories, these CWE numbers are the standard language.
key_concepts.ai_hint: CWE-89 = SQL injection. CWE-78 = Command injection. Both have the same root cause: mixing user input with command structure.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Vulnerable Login (The Classic Attack)
complete_examples.description: A login system that builds SQL with string concatenation. We trace exactly how an attacker bypasses authentication without knowing the password.
complete_examples.output:
=== Normal Login ===
Query sent to database:
SELECT * FROM members WHERE u='alice' AND p='alice123'
Result: true

=== SQL Injection Attack ===
Query sent to database:
SELECT * FROM members WHERE u='admin' AND p='' OR '1'='1'
Result: true
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line by line:
- Line 14-15: The query is built by GLUING the user input directly into the SQL string using +
- Normal case: u='alice' AND p='alice123' - works correctly, checks both conditions
- Attack case: The password ' OR '1'='1 gets inserted, creating: p='' OR '1'='1'
  - The ' closes the password string early (empty password)
  - OR adds a new condition
  - '1'='1' is ALWAYS true
  - So the WHERE clause becomes TRUE regardless of the actual password
- Line 19: rs.next() returns true because rows were found
- The attacker is logged in as admin without knowing the password!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try changing the attack payload to: admin' -- (with a space before --). What happens? The -- comments out everything after it, including the password check entirely!

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Secure Login with PreparedStatement
complete_examples.description: The same login system, now protected with PreparedStatement. The exact same attack payload fails completely.
complete_examples.output:
=== Normal Login ===
Query template: SELECT * FROM members WHERE u = ? AND p = ?
Parameter 1: alice
Parameter 2: alice123
Result: true

=== SQL Injection Attempt (BLOCKED) ===
Query template: SELECT * FROM members WHERE u = ? AND p = ?
Parameter 1: admin
Parameter 2: ' OR '1'='1
Result: false
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line by line:
- Line 13-14: The SQL template has ? placeholders - the STRUCTURE is fixed
- Line 15-16: setString() binds the values as DATA, not code
- The database receives two separate things: the query structure and the parameter values
- When the attacker sends ' OR '1'='1 as the password, the database searches for a user whose password is literally the string: ' OR '1'='1
- No such password exists, so zero rows are returned
- rs.next() returns false - the attack is blocked!
- The ' character is treated as just a character in the data, not as SQL syntax
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Notice we also changed the error message on line 29 to a generic 'Database error' instead of showing e.getMessage(). Why? Because SQL error messages can reveal table names and column names to attackers!

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Numeric Injection - Product Lookup
complete_examples.description: A product search that expects a number but does not validate. An attacker can dump all products or even other tables.
complete_examples.output:
=== Normal Request ===
Query: SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id = 42
WidgetX Pro - $29.99

=== Dump All Products ===
Query: SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id = 42 OR 1=1
WidgetX Pro - $29.99
GadgetY Basic - $9.99
SuperZ Premium - $149.99
... (all products listed)

=== UNION Attack ===
Query: SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id = 42 UNION SELECT username, password FROM users --
WidgetX Pro - $29.99
admin - s3cretP@ss
alice - alice123
bob - b0bPass!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The product lookup expects a number (42) but never validates it.

Attack 1 (OR 1=1): No quotes needed because it is a numeric field. Adding OR 1=1 makes the WHERE clause always true, dumping all products.

Attack 2 (UNION SELECT): The attacker combines the product query with a query against the users table. The UNION merges both result sets. Now usernames appear in the 'name' column and passwords appear in the 'price' column. All credentials exposed!

Fix: Use PreparedStatement with setInt() and validate that the input is actually a number.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the attacker used: 42; DROP TABLE products -- ? Would this work? (Hint: it depends on whether the database allows multiple statements in one query.)

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Command Injection via DNS Lookup
complete_examples.description: A web application that performs DNS lookups by calling nslookup through the shell. An attacker injects extra commands.
complete_examples.output:
=== VULNERABLE ===
Server: 192.168.1.1
Address: 192.168.1.1#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name: google.com
Address: 142.250.187.206

root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
daemon:x:1:1:daemon:/usr/sbin:/usr/sbin/nologin
... (entire /etc/passwd file exposed!)

=== SECURE ===
** server can't find google.com;: NXDOMAIN
(nslookup treats the entire string including ; as the domain name)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Vulnerable version: Uses /bin/sh -c which invokes the shell. The shell sees the ; and treats it as a command separator. It runs nslookup google.com THEN runs cat /etc/passwd. Both commands execute!

Secure version: Uses the array form of exec(). No shell is involved. The entire string 'google.com; cat /etc/passwd' is passed as ONE argument to nslookup. nslookup tries to look up that weird domain name, fails, and the ; is never interpreted as a command separator.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Even better than the array form: use Java's InetAddress.getByName(domain) for DNS lookups. This removes the external command entirely. No command = no command injection!

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Second-Order SQL Injection
complete_examples.description: A sneaky attack where the injection payload is stored in the database first, then triggered later when the stored data is used in another query.
complete_examples.output:
Attacker registers with username:
admin' OR '1'='1' --

Later, when profile is viewed:
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer = 'admin' OR '1'='1' --'
= Returns ALL orders from ALL customers!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This is sneaky! The registration uses PreparedStatement (safe), so the malicious username admin' OR '1'='1' -- is stored in the database as plain text. No injection happens during registration.

But later, when viewProfile() reads the username from the database and concatenates it into ANOTHER query using Statement (line 30-31), the stored payload activates.

The lesson: NEVER trust data from the database just because it was stored safely. Always use PreparedStatement for EVERY query, even when the data comes from your own database.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: How would you fix the viewProfile() method? Replace the Statement with PreparedStatement and use ? for the username parameter.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Multiple Injection Payloads Compared
complete_examples.description: Eight different SQL injection payloads against the same vulnerable login, showing how each one works differently.
complete_examples.output:
Attack: Classic OR
User:   admin
Pass:   ' OR '1'='1
Query:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' AND password='' OR '1'='1'

Attack: Comment (--)
User:   admin'--
Pass:   anything
Query:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin'--' AND password='anything'

Attack: Always True
User:   ' OR 1=1 --
Pass:   anything
Query:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' OR 1=1 --' AND password='anything'

Attack: UNION Steal
User:   ' UNION SELECT username,password FROM users --
Pass:   x
Query:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' UNION SELECT username,password FROM users --' AND password='x'

Attack: DROP TABLE
User:   '; DROP TABLE users --
Pass:   x
Query:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username=''; DROP TABLE users --' AND password='x'

Attack: Add Admin
User:   '; INSERT INTO users VALUES('hacker','pass123') --
Pass:   x
Query:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username=''; INSERT INTO users VALUES('hacker','pass123') --' AND password='x'

Attack: Time Blind
User:   admin' AND SLEEP(5) --
Pass:   x
Query:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' AND SLEEP(5) --' AND password='x'

Attack: Boolean Blind
User:   admin' AND SUBSTRING(password,1,1)='s' --
Pass:   x
Query:  SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' AND SUBSTRING(password,1,1)='s' --' AND password='x'
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Each payload uses the same vulnerability (string concatenation) but achieves different goals:

1. Classic OR: Makes the WHERE always true -> returns all rows
2. Comment: Uses -- to comment out the password check entirely
3. Always True: Combines empty string match with OR 1=1
4. UNION Steal: Appends a second query to extract usernames and passwords
5. DROP TABLE: Terminates the first query with ; then drops the table
6. Add Admin: Creates a new user account for the attacker
7. Time Blind: Makes the database sleep 5 seconds if admin exists
8. Boolean Blind: Extracts password one character at a time

All eight attacks are blocked by using PreparedStatement.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Pick any three payloads and explain in your own words: (1) What does the single quote do? (2) What does -- do? (3) What is the attacker trying to achieve?

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Defence Layer - Result Count Verification
complete_examples.description: Even with PreparedStatement, adding result verification catches anomalies that might indicate an attack or bug.
complete_examples.output:
Normal login: expects 0 or 1 rows
If OR 1=1 somehow bypassed PreparedStatement:
  -> Would return ALL rows (e.g., 500)
  -> rowCount > 1 triggers SECURITY ALERT
  -> Login DENIED + incident logged
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This adds a safety net. A login query should return exactly 0 or 1 rows. If it returns more than 1, either the database has duplicate entries (a bug) or an injection attack succeeded (a security breach). Either way, denying access and alerting the security team is the correct response.

This is defence in depth: even if PreparedStatement somehow fails (unlikely but possible with complex queries), the result verification catches the anomaly.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add similar verification to a product search: if a search by ID returns more than 5 results, log a warning. Why 5 and not 1? Because a product search might legitimately return several results.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Whitelisting for Non-Parameterisable Values
complete_examples.description: Table names and ORDER BY clauses cannot use ? parameters. Whitelisting is the correct defence.
complete_examples.output:
=== Vulnerable ===
table='users; DROP TABLE users --'
Query: SELECT * FROM users; DROP TABLE users -- ORDER BY name
Result: TABLE DELETED!

=== Secure (Whitelist) ===
table='users; DROP TABLE users --'
Result: SecurityException - Invalid table!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
PreparedStatement cannot parameterise table names, column names, or ORDER BY clauses. For these, whitelisting is the answer.

The whitelist contains ONLY the values you expect. Any input that does not exactly match a whitelist entry is rejected immediately. The attacker cannot bypass this because they cannot add new entries to your whitelist.

Key rule: if you cannot use ?, use a whitelist.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a whitelist for sort direction: only allow 'ASC' or 'DESC'. What happens if the attacker sends 'ASC; DROP TABLE users --' as the direction?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Stored Procedure Defence
complete_examples.description: Using stored procedures to reduce the attack surface. The application calls a procedure by name, never builds raw SQL.
complete_examples.output:
Benefits of Stored Procedures:
1. SQL is in the database, not the application
2. Application only calls by name with parameters
3. Database controls what operations are allowed
4. Reduced attack surface
5. Can enforce additional security logic in DB

The application never sees raw SQL.
Even if the app is compromised, the attacker
can only call procedures that already exist.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Stored procedures move the SQL logic INTO the database. The Java application never writes SQL - it just calls a procedure by name with parameters. This is like having a locked menu at a restaurant: you can only order items that exist on the menu, you cannot write your own orders.

The CallableStatement works like PreparedStatement - parameters are bound separately from the procedure call structure. The database handles the rest internally.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the stored procedure itself is written with string concatenation inside? (Hint: then the stored procedure is vulnerable! The fix must be consistent all the way down.)

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Database Least Privilege Configuration
complete_examples.description: Shows how to configure database permissions so that even a successful injection causes minimal damage.
complete_examples.output:
=== BAD: Application uses admin account ===
Connection: jdbc:mysql://db?user=root&password=admin123
Permissions: ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.*
If SQL injection succeeds, attacker can:
  - Read ANY table in ANY database
  - Modify or delete ANY data
  - Create new admin accounts
  - Drop entire databases
  - Execute OS commands via xp_cmdshell

=== GOOD: Separate accounts per function ===

Login Service Account:
  CREATE USER 'app_login'@'localhost';
  GRANT SELECT ON myapp.users TO 'app_login'@'localhost';
  -> Can ONLY read users table. Nothing else.

Product Service Account:
  CREATE USER 'app_products'@'localhost';
  GRANT SELECT ON myapp.products TO 'app_products'@'localhost';
  GRANT SELECT ON myapp.categories TO 'app_products'@'localhost';
  -> Can only read products and categories.

Order Service Account:
  CREATE USER 'app_orders'@'localhost';
  GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON myapp.orders TO 'app_orders'@'localhost';
  -> Can read and create orders. Cannot delete.

If login service is injected:
  Attacker can ONLY read users table
  Cannot access products, orders, or admin
  Cannot DROP or DELETE anything
  Damage is CONTAINED
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Many organisations use the database root/admin account for their application. This means if SQL injection succeeds, the attacker has UNLIMITED database access.

Least privilege means creating SEPARATE database accounts for each part of your application, each with ONLY the permissions it needs. The login page only needs to SELECT from the users table. The product browser only needs to SELECT from products.

This limits the blast radius: even if injection succeeds, the attacker can only do what that specific account is allowed to do.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Design the database permissions for a blog application: what would the reader account, author account, and admin account each be allowed to do?

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Secure Command Execution with ProcessBuilder
complete_examples.description: The modern, safer way to run external commands in Java using ProcessBuilder without shell interpretation.
complete_examples.output:
=== API (no command at all) ===
IP: 142.250.187.206
Lookup failed

=== ProcessBuilder (no shell) ===
Safe: (nslookup output for google.com)
Attack: ** server can't find google.com;: NXDOMAIN

=== Validated + ProcessBuilder ===
Attack: Invalid domain format
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Three layers of defence for command execution:

1. API first: Use Java's built-in InetAddress instead of calling nslookup. No external command = no command injection possible.

2. ProcessBuilder with separate arguments: If you must use an external command, use ProcessBuilder with each argument as a separate string. The ; in the input is treated as part of the domain name, not as a command separator.

3. Input validation: Before even reaching the command, validate that the domain matches an expected pattern. The regex rejects anything containing ; | & or other metacharacters.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if you need to run a command that REQUIRES a shell (like using pipes)? The answer: redesign your approach to not need a shell. If truly impossible, create a whitelist of allowed complete commands.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Complete Secure CRUD Application
complete_examples.description: A full Create-Read-Update-Delete application using PreparedStatement everywhere, with input validation and error handling.
complete_examples.output:
Secure CRUD Pattern:
1. Validate input BEFORE database operations
2. Use PreparedStatement for ALL queries
3. Use setString/setInt (never concatenate)
4. Verify affected row counts
5. Log anomalies for security monitoring
6. Show generic errors to users
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This example combines EVERY defence technique into a complete application:

- Input validation with regex (whitelisting)
- PreparedStatement with ? for ALL queries (even simple ones)
- setString() and setInt() for type-safe parameter binding
- Row count verification (checks that updates/deletes affect expected number of rows)
- Secure logging (logs anomalies without exposing to users)
- Generic error messages (no database details leaked)

This is the pattern every Java database application should follow.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Extend this with a search function: searchUsers(String keyword) that searches by username with LIKE '%keyword%'. How would you make this safe with PreparedStatement? (Hint: you CAN use ? with LIKE)

complete_examples.title: Example 13: sqlmap Automated Attack Simulation
complete_examples.description: Understanding how automated tools like sqlmap systematically exploit injection vulnerabilities.
complete_examples.output:
=== How sqlmap Works (Conceptual) ===

Step 1: Detection - Find injection points
  Sends: id=1'     -> Error? Possible SQLi!
  Sends: id=1 AND 1=1  -> Normal response?
  Sends: id=1 AND 1=2  -> Different response?
  If responses differ -> CONFIRMED injectable!

Step 2: Fingerprint - Identify the database
  Sends: id=1 AND 'a'='a   -> MySQL syntax
  Sends: id=1; SELECT 1    -> PostgreSQL syntax
  Checks error messages for database version

Step 3: Enumerate - Map the database
  Extracts: database names
  Extracts: table names in each database
  Extracts: column names in each table
  Extracts: row count for each table

Step 4: Dump - Extract all data
  Uses UNION or blind techniques
  Extracts data row by row, column by column
  Can dump ENTIRE database in minutes!

Step 5: Escalate - Go beyond the database
  --os-shell: Get operating system shell
  --file-read: Read server files
  --file-write: Write files to server

Total time for complete database dump: 5-30 minutes
Total human effort: One command line
Defence: PreparedStatement blocks ALL of these.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Automated tools like sqlmap turn SQL injection from a skill into a button press. The tool systematically tests every input parameter, determines the database type, maps the entire schema, and extracts all data - often in under 30 minutes.

This is why SQL injection is so dangerous: the attacker does not need to be an expert. They just need to find ONE injection point and let the tool do the rest.

Good news: PreparedStatement blocks every technique sqlmap uses, because the tool relies on changing the query structure - which parameterised queries prevent entirely.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If you were a security tester, how would you defend against automated scanning? (Hint: WAF rules, rate limiting, and monitoring for unusual query patterns.)

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Error-Based Information Leakage
complete_examples.description: How SQL error messages reveal database structure to attackers, enabling more targeted injection attacks.
complete_examples.output:
=== VULNERABLE: Error Leakage ===
Input: ' UNION SELECT 1,2,3 --
Error: SQLite error: SELECTs to the left and right
       of UNION do not have the same number of
       result columns (4 vs 3)
  -> Attacker now knows: table has 4 columns!

=== SECURE: Generic Error ===
Input: ' UNION SELECT 1,2,3 --
Please enter a valid product number.
  -> Attacker learns NOTHING.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Error messages are goldmines for attackers. From SQL errors, they can learn: the database type (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite), table and column names, the number of columns in a query, and the SQL syntax being used.

This example shows how an attacker uses error messages to determine column count for a UNION attack. The fix: NEVER show database errors to users. Log them internally with a reference ID, and show a generic message to the user.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: An application shows the error: 'Column user_password not found in table tbl_accounts'. What three pieces of information did the attacker just learn?

visual_diagram.title: SQL Injection: Attack Flow & Defence Architecture
visual_diagram.content:
SQL INJECTION: ATTACK vs DEFENCE
VULNERABLE PATH (String Concatenation) 
  User Types: admin' OR '1'='1
       |
       v
  Java Application
  String query = "SELECT * FROM users
    WHERE u='" + user + "' AND p='" + pass + "'";
       |
       v
  MIXED TOGETHER: Code + Data = ONE STRING
  SELECT * FROM users
  WHERE u='admin' OR '1'='1'
          ^               ^
     legit data      INJECTED CODE
       |
       v
  Database executes EVERYTHING
  OR '1'='1' is always TRUE
       |
       v
  ALL ROWS RETURNED - LOGIN BYPASSED!
SECURE PATH (PreparedStatement) 
  Same User Types: admin' OR '1'='1
       |
       v
  Java Application
  PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement(
    "SELECT * FROM users WHERE u=? AND p=?");
  ps.setString(1, user);  // Bound as DATA
  ps.setString(2, pass);  // Bound as DATA
       |
       v
  KEPT SEPARATE: Structure and Data travel apart
  Structure: SELECT * FROM users WHERE u=? AND p=?
  Param 1:   "admin"          (just data)
  Param 2:   "' OR '1'='1"   (just data)
       |
       v
  Database searches for literal string
  username = "admin" AND password = "' OR '1'='1"
       |
       v
  ZERO ROWS - No such password - ATTACK BLOCKED!
COMMAND INJECTION: Same Concept, Different Target 
  VULNERABLE:
  Input: google.com; cat /etc/passwd
       |
       v
  Runtime.exec("/bin/sh -c nslookup " + input)
  Shell sees: nslookup google.com; cat /etc/passwd
  Runs BOTH commands!
       |
       v
  System files exposed!

  SECURE:
  Input: google.com; cat /etc/passwd
       |
       v
  Runtime.exec(new String[]{"nslookup", input})
  nslookup receives: "google.com; cat /etc/passwd" as ONE argument
  Tries to look up that "domain", fails harmlessly
       |
       v
  Attack blocked! No shell = no metacharacter interpretation
DEFENCE IN DEPTH - 6 LAYERS 
  Layer 1 PreparedStatement (parameterised queries)
    Separates code from data. Blocks 99% of attacks.

  Layer 2 Input Validation (whitelisting)
    Only allow expected characters: [a-zA-Z0-9_]
    Reject everything else BEFORE it reaches the query.

  Layer 3 Least Privilege (database permissions)
    Login service: SELECT only on users table.
    Product service: SELECT only on products table.
    If injected, attacker can only do what that account allows.

  Layer 4 Result Verification
    Login returns >1 row? ALERT! Expected 0 or 1.
    Delete affects >1 row? ROLLBACK! Something is wrong.

  Layer 5 Stored Procedures
    SQL lives in the database, not the application.
    App only calls procedures by name with parameters.

  Layer 6 Error Handling
    NEVER show SQL errors to users.
    Log internally with reference ID.
    Generic message: "An error occurred. Ref: ERR-12345"
8 COMMON SQL INJECTION PAYLOADS 
  1. Classic OR:     ' OR '1'='1
     Makes WHERE always true, returns all rows

  2. Comment:        admin'--
     Comments out password check entirely

  3. Always True:    ' OR 1=1 --
     Universal bypass, works on most databases

  4. UNION:          ' UNION SELECT user,pass FROM users --
     Steals data from other tables

  5. DROP TABLE:     '; DROP TABLE users --
     Destroys the table completely

  6. INSERT:         '; INSERT INTO users VALUES('hacker','pw') --
     Creates a backdoor admin account

  7. Time Blind:     ' AND SLEEP(5) --
     If page delays 5s, injection confirmed

  8. Boolean Blind:  ' AND SUBSTRING(pass,1,1)='a' --
     Extracts data character by character
REMEMBER - THE 5 GOLDEN RULES 
  Rule 1: ALWAYS use PreparedStatement. NEVER concatenate SQL with +
  Rule 2: Validate input with whitelisting, not blacklisting
  Rule 3: Use least privilege database accounts
  Rule 4: NEVER show database errors to users
  Rule 5: For OS commands, use APIs or array-based exec (no shell)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Predict the SQL Query Result
practice_exercises.description: Given a users table and a SQL query, predict what rows are returned.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let us start with the basics! Before we can understand SQL injection, we need to understand what SQL queries normally do. Look at the table and query below, then predict which rows are returned.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Row 1: name = Alice, salary = 85000
Row 2: name = Carol, salary = 92000
Total rows returned: 2

hints.message: Look at the WHERE clause: department = 'Engineering'. Which rows have Engineering as their department?
Alice (row 1) and Carol (row 3) are both in Engineering. The query only asks for name and salary columns.
Answer: Alice (85000) and Carol (92000). Two rows returned. The SELECT only picks name and salary, not all columns.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Write a SELECT Query
practice_exercises.description: Write a SQL query to find all products under $20.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now YOU write the SQL! This is important because to inject SQL, you need to understand how normal SQL works first.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price < 20
Result: USB Cable (9.99), Notebook (4.99), Mouse (19.99), Pen Set (12.99)
4 rows returned

hints.message: Start with SELECT * FROM products, then add WHERE with a price condition.
The condition is: price < 20. So: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price < 20
Full answer: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price < 20. Returns 4 rows: USB Cable, Notebook, Mouse, and Pen Set.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Trace a Query with AND
practice_exercises.description: Given a query with AND, determine which rows match BOTH conditions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep building your SQL skills! Understanding normal queries is the foundation for understanding attacks.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Alice (age 28, active true) and Dave (age 41, active true)

hints.message: AND means BOTH conditions must be true. Check each row: age > 25 AND active = true.
Bob is age 35 but active=false (fails AND). Carol is age 22 (fails age > 25).
Answer: Alice (28, true) and Dave (41, true). Both satisfy BOTH conditions.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Trace a Query with OR
practice_exercises.description: Given a query with OR, determine which rows match EITHER condition.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep building your SQL skills! Understanding normal queries is the foundation for understanding attacks.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Book (paid), Laptop (amount > 20 AND paid), Mouse (amount > 20). 3 items.

hints.message: OR means EITHER condition can be true. Check amount > 20 OR status = 'paid'.
Book: amount=15 (no) but status=paid (yes) -> included. Pen: amount=3 (no) and status=unpaid (no) -> excluded.
Answer: Book (paid), Laptop (both), Mouse (amount > 20). Pen excluded (neither condition true).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Write an INSERT Statement
practice_exercises.description: Write SQL to add a new employee to the database.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep building your SQL skills! Understanding normal queries is the foundation for understanding attacks.
practice_exercises.expected_output: INSERT INTO employees (name, department, salary) VALUES ('Frank', 'Engineering', 78000)

hints.message: INSERT INTO table (columns) VALUES (values). Match the column order.
INSERT INTO employees (name, department, salary) VALUES ('Frank', 'Engineering', 78000)
Note: String values need single quotes. Numbers do not. Column order must match VALUES order.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Write an UPDATE Statement
practice_exercises.description: Write SQL to change an employee's salary.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep building your SQL skills! Understanding normal queries is the foundation for understanding attacks.
practice_exercises.expected_output: UPDATE employees SET salary = 90000 WHERE name = 'Alice'

hints.message: UPDATE table SET column = new_value WHERE condition.
UPDATE employees SET salary = 90000 WHERE name = 'Alice'
ALWAYS include WHERE! Without it, ALL employees would get salary 90000.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Write a DELETE Statement
practice_exercises.description: Write SQL to remove a terminated employee's record.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep building your SQL skills! Understanding normal queries is the foundation for understanding attacks.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 4
Without WHERE: ALL users would be deleted!

hints.message: DELETE FROM table WHERE condition. What is the condition for id 4?
DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 4
Without WHERE: DELETE FROM users deletes ALL users! This is a common and catastrophic mistake.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Predict the Result of LIKE
practice_exercises.description: Given a query using LIKE '%search%', predict what matches.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep building your SQL skills! Understanding normal queries is the foundation for understanding attacks.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Wireless Mouse and USB Mouse (both contain 'Mouse')

hints.message: LIKE '%Mouse%' means: contains 'Mouse' anywhere in the string. The % is a wildcard.
Check each: 'Wireless Mouse' contains Mouse (yes), 'Wireless Keyboard' (no), 'USB Mouse' (yes), 'Monitor' (no).
Answer: Wireless Mouse and USB Mouse. The % matches any characters before and after 'Mouse'.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Understand ORDER BY
practice_exercises.description: Given a query with ORDER BY, arrange results correctly.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep building your SQL skills! Understanding normal queries is the foundation for understanding attacks.

hints.message: ORDER BY grade DESC means highest grade first. Sort the numbers: 95, 92, 88, 87, 78.
Dave(95), Bob(92), Eve(88), Alice(87), Carol(78)
DESC = descending (high to low). ASC = ascending (low to high). Default is ASC if not specified.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Trace a JOIN Query
practice_exercises.description: Given two tables, trace what a JOIN returns.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep building your SQL skills! Understanding normal queries is the foundation for understanding attacks.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Alice, $50
2. Bob, $30
3. Alice, $25
(Alice appears twice because she has 2 orders)

hints.message: JOIN matches rows where customers.id = orders.customer_id.
Customer 1 (Alice) has 2 orders (101 and 103). Customer 2 (Bob) has 1 order (102). Customer 3 (Carol) has 0 orders (not in result).
Answer: Alice $50, Bob $30, Alice $25. Alice appears twice because JOIN produces one row per matching pair.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Spot the Vulnerable Line
practice_exercises.description: Identify which line of code creates the SQL injection vulnerability.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Line 4 is vulnerable.
String concatenation (+) mixes user input with SQL.
Replace with PreparedStatement using ? parameters.

hints.message: Look for where user input (the 'name' parameter) meets SQL string building.
Line 4 uses + to concatenate the name variable directly into the SQL string.
Line 4. Use PreparedStatement: conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username=?"); then ps.setString(1, name);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Trace the Classic OR Attack
practice_exercises.description: Trace character-by-character how ' OR '1'='1 bypasses authentication.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' AND password='' OR '1'='1'
Condition 1: username = 'admin'
Condition 2: password = '' (empty)
Condition 3: '1' = '1' (always TRUE)
Operator: OR
Result: TRUE (OR makes it true)
Rows returned: ALL

hints.message: Start by writing the query with both inputs substituted in.
The password ' OR '1'='1 closes the password string early with the first quote, then adds OR.
Full query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' AND password='' OR '1'='1'. The OR makes the entire WHERE true.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Trace the Comment Attack
practice_exercises.description: Trace how admin'-- skips the password check entirely.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin'--' AND password='anything'
-- is a SQL comment. Everything after it is ignored.
The password check AND password='anything' is commented out.
Password NOT checked. Login as admin: YES.

hints.message: Replace [U] with admin'-- in the query template. What happens to the single quote?
The ' after admin closes the username string. The -- starts a comment.
Query becomes: ...WHERE username='admin'--' AND password='anything'. Everything after -- is a comment, so password is never checked!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Craft Your Own Bypass Payload
practice_exercises.description: Write a SQL injection payload that returns all users from a login form.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Payload 1: u=admin, p=' OR '1'='1
Payload 2: u=admin'--, p=anything
Payload 3: u=' OR 1=1 --, p=anything

hints.message: For OR approach: the password field needs to close the quote and add OR true.
For comment approach: the username field needs to close the quote and start a comment.
Payload 1: p=' OR '1'='1. Payload 2: u=admin'-- p=anything. Payload 3: u=' OR 1=1 -- p=anything (bypass without knowing any username).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Fix the Vulnerable Login
practice_exercises.description: Rewrite the vulnerable login to use PreparedStatement.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.

hints.message: Replace Statement with PreparedStatement. Replace string concatenation with ? placeholders.
conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username=? AND password=?")

Full code: PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username=? AND password=?"); ps.setString(1, user); ps.setString(2, pass); ResultSet rs = ps.executeQuery(); return rs.next();

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Multiple Payload Comparison
practice_exercises.description: Given 5 payloads, predict which ones successfully bypass login.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. BYPASS (OR '1'='1' always true)
2. BYPASS (OR 'a'='a' also always true)
3. FAIL (AND '1'='1' doesnt help - both sides must be true)
4. BYPASS (-- comments out password)
5. BYPASS (1=1 is true, -- comments rest)
#3 fails because AND requires password='' to be true AND '1'='1' to be true. Since there's no password match, it fails.

hints.message: For each, substitute the values and check if the WHERE clause evaluates to TRUE.
#3 uses AND instead of OR. AND '1'='1' does not help because the password still must match.
1:BYPASS 2:BYPASS 3:FAIL 4:BYPASS 5:BYPASS. The key: OR creates an alternative true condition, AND does not.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Injection on a Search Form
practice_exercises.description: Exploit SQL injection in a product search feature.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Return all: Input: %' OR '1'='1' --
Query: SELECT * FROM products WHERE name LIKE '%%' OR '1'='1' --'

With UNION: Input: %' UNION SELECT username,password,null FROM users --
Query: SELECT * FROM products WHERE name LIKE '%%' UNION SELECT username,password,null FROM users --'

hints.message: In a LIKE clause, close the pattern with %' then add your injection.
Input: %' OR '1'='1' -- This closes the LIKE pattern and adds an always-true condition.
For UNION: %' UNION SELECT username,password,null FROM users -- (you need the right number of null columns to match).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Second-Order Injection Detection
practice_exercises.description: Identify where a stored value triggers a second-order injection.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Q1: Yes, registration is safe (uses PreparedStatement)
Q2: Malicious username: admin' OR '1'='1' --
Q3: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer='admin' OR '1'='1' --'
Q4: Use PreparedStatement for the orders query too!

hints.message: The registration stores the username safely. But where is the username USED later?

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Bypass a Simple Blacklist Filter
practice_exercises.description: Given a filter that removes 'OR', craft a payload that still works.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Payload: ' OOR '1'='1
After filter: ' OR '1'='1 (the filter removes 'OR' from 'OOR', leaving 'O' + 'R' = 'OR'!)
Query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE u='admin' AND p='' OR '1'='1'
The blacklist is bypassed!

hints.message: The filter removes 'OR'. What if your input contains 'OOR'?
After removing 'OR' from 'OOR', you get 'O' wait... 'OOR' minus 'OR' = 'O'? No! 'OOR' -> the filter finds 'OR' inside it and removes it, leaving just 'O'.
Try: ' OORR '1'='1. Filter removes 'OR' from 'OORR' leaving 'OR'. The payload works! This is why blacklisting always fails.

hints.message: The attack closes the comment value with '), then adds a new SQL statement.
comment = '); DROP TABLE feedback -- makes the query: VALUES ('Alice', ''); DROP TABLE feedback --')
The first query inserts with empty comment. The second query DROPS the table. Fix with PreparedStatement.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: List Dangerous Shell Metacharacters

|| -> run second only if first fails

$() -> command substitution (same as backtick)
> -> redirect output to file (overwrite)
< -> redirect input from file

Full timeline: Block IP -> Check DB logs -> Assess data exposure -> Find vulnerable code -> Fix with PreparedStatement -> Review all code -> Notify if needed.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Build an Injection Test Plan
practice_exercises.description: Apply injection knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These are the scenarios you will face in real jobs. Every security professional needs to handle these situations.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Comprehensive test plan covering all input fields, multiple payload types, expected blocked behaviours.

hints.message: For each component, think: what input fields exist? What SQL do they touch?
Test payloads: ' OR 1=1 --, admin'--, ' UNION SELECT null,null --, ; DROP TABLE --, and numeric variants.
A successful attack looks like: unexpected data returned, login without password, error messages revealing database structure.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Capstone: Secure Banking Application
practice_exercises.description: Apply injection knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These are the scenarios you will face in real jobs. Every security professional needs to handle these situations.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Complete implementation with all 7 security layers for a banking transfer system.

hints.message: Start with input validation: account numbers should match a pattern, amount must be positive and within limits.
Use transactions: BEGIN -> check balance -> debit sender -> credit receiver -> COMMIT (or ROLLBACK on any error).
Full implementation: validate all inputs, PreparedStatement for all queries, transaction management, balance check, audit log entry, generic error messages, result verification.
